explanations.8: The UID variable is set to the host user's ID if provided, otherwise defaults to 1000; this ensures files created in the container are owned by your user, not root.
explanations.9: Same idea as UID but for the group ID, so the container process runs as a regular user instead of root for security.
explanations.11: The current directory is mounted into /workspace with 'cached' mode, which speeds up file access on Mac/Windows but means the container's view of files might lag slightly behind your actual edits.
explanations.13: This creates a temporary /tmp folder inside the container with a 64MB size limit and restrictions (noexec means no executables can run from there, nosuid blocks special permissions) to prevent misuse.
explanations.15: ALL capabilities are being dropped, meaning the container loses most Linux power-user abilities—this is a security hardening measure to prevent container breakout attacks.
explanations.18: This prevents the container from gaining new privileges even if it somehow exploits a vulnerability, adding another layer of security.
explanations.19: The container's filesystem is read-only, so the process can't modify files on disk except in the writable tmpfs areas defined above—this prevents accidental (or malicious) changes to your code.
